metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py` to the Scope with a concrete change: update `PlanSeverityAttrs.ac_unverifiable` from `str` to a `Literal['none','underspecified_oracle','broken_oracle','missing_oracle']` type (or a Pydantic validator) so the closed-set rejection at write time is actually enforced by the Pydantic model, not just described in the prompt.
evidence: Plan Scope lists: decide.py, workflow_ops.py, plan_review_verifier.md, plan_review_verifier_agentic.md, sidecar.py, unit tests, docs — but NOT verify.py
AC1: 'the plan-review verification output schema accepts exactly {none, underspecified_oracle, broken_oracle, missing_oracle} for the axis and rejects anything else at write time'
verify.py line 259: `ac_unverifiable: str = Field(default='none', description='none|low|medium|high ...')` — currently a bare `str`, no Literal or closed-set validator
passes.py line 207: `contracts.register_contract('plan_review_verification', _pass2_model)` — the registered Pydantic model is the enforcement point
The `strict=True` mode (verify.py line 201-203) controls extra fields, not the value vocabulary of existing fields — it does not enforce a closed set for `ac_unverifiable`'s string value
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'the plan-review verification output schema accepts exactly {none, underspecified_oracle, broken_oracle, missing_oracle} for the axis and rejects anything else at write time,' but the write-time enforcement mechanism lives in `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py`'s `PlanSeverityAttrs` class — which currently defines `ac_unverifiable: str = Field(default='none', ...)` with no Literal type or closed-set validator. This file is NOT in the plan's Scope. The prompt-level definitions in the two `.md` files cannot enforce schema rejection at write time; only the Pydantic model in `verify.py` can. The plan's assertion about where closed-set enforcement happens is contradicted by the code structure.
findings.location: Scope section / AC1 ('rejects anything else at write time') / src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py (absent from Scope)
scenarios: An LLM verifier emits `ac_unverifiable: 'low'` on a plan-v3 run — the Pydantic model accepts it silently (str field), the old-vocabulary grade passes through, impact_plan scores it via _SEV01 (producing 0.33) rather than ORACLE_GRADE01, and the plan-v3 hard-floor fires when it should not. The 'rejection at write time' never happens.
findings.suggested_fix: In `verify.py`'s `plan_review_verification_model`, change the `ac_unverifiable` field from `str = Field(default='none')` to `Literal['none', 'underspecified_oracle', 'broken_oracle', 'missing_oracle'] = Field(default='none')` (when `strict=True`) or add a `field_validator` that rejects out-of-vocabulary values on plan-v3 writes. Add `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py` to the Scope.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py` to Scope: update `PlanSeverityAttrs.ac_unverifiable` Field description and any validator to accept exactly `{none, underspecified_oracle, broken_oracle, missing_oracle}` and reject the old `low|medium|high` vocabulary on plan-v3 verifications (this is the write-time enforcement AC1 requires).
evidence: Plan Scope lists: decide.py, workflow_ops.py, plan_review_verifier.md, plan_review_verifier_agentic.md, sidecar.py, unit tests, docs — verify.py is absent.

AC1: 'The verification model accepts exactly the closed grade set {none, underspecified_oracle, broken_oracle, missing_oracle} for ac_unverifiable on new plan-v3 verifications — pinned by BOTH an acceptance test and a rejection test for an out-of-vocabulary grade'
verify.py line 259: `ac_unverifiable: str = Field(default='none', description='none|low|medium|high — ...')`

The strict-mode schema (`strict=True` → `ConfigDict(extra='forbid')`) passed to `plan_review_verification_model()` enforces valid values at write time; the closed-set validation for a str field's allowed values is specified in the Field description and enforced by the validator registered in `passes.py`.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py` is absent from Scope but owns `PlanSeverityAttrs.ac_unverifiable`, the Pydantic field whose `description` and effective closed-set enforcement (in strict mode, `ConfigDict(extra='forbid')`) must change from `none|low|medium|high` to `{none, underspecified_oracle, broken_oracle, missing_oracle}`. AC1 requires the schema to accept exactly the new grade set and reject out-of-vocabulary values at write time — that enforcement lives in this file, not in the prompt texts or `decide.py`.
findings.location: Scope section; `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/verify.py` lines 259-263 (`ac_unverifiable` Field definition in `PlanSeverityAttrs`)
scenarios: The verifier LLM emits `underspecified_oracle` for ac_unverifiable; if PlanSeverityAttrs still expects `none|low|medium|high`, strict-mode Pydantic validation rejects the output and the finding degrades to INDETERMINATE, silently defeating the whole change.

The acceptance/rejection test for out-of-vocabulary grades (AC1) must instantiate `plan_review_verification_model(strict=True)` with the new vocab — if the Field is unchanged, the old vocab 'low' would still be accepted and the new 'underspecified_oracle' would still be rejected, inverting the intended behavior.
findings.suggested_fix: In `verify.py`'s `PlanSeverityAttrs`, change the `ac_unverifiable` field to: `ac_unverifiable: str = Field(default='none', description='none|underspecified_oracle|broken_oracle|missing_oracle — ...')` and add a `@field_validator('ac_unverifiable')` (or equivalent) that coerces/rejects values outside the new closed set in strict mode.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly in the Scope/What section that `impact_plan` must dispatch through `ORACLE_GRADE01` (not `_SEV01`) for the `ac_unverifiable` slot in BOTH the contribs-list iteration over `_PLAN_SEVERITY_AXES` AND the `has_override` predicate over `_PLAN_HARD_OVERRIDE_AXES`, and add a unit-test pin confirming `impact_plan({'ac_unverifiable': 'broken_oracle'}) >= 0.85`.
evidence: Plan: 'ORACLE_GRADE01 = {none: 0.0, underspecified_oracle: 0.55, broken_oracle: 1.0, missing_oracle: 1.0} — used for BOTH the ac_unverifiable MAX contribution AND the hard-override detection'
decide.py line 166: `contribs = [_SEV01.get(attrs.get(a), 0.0) for a in _PLAN_SEVERITY_AXES]` — ac_unverifiable is in _PLAN_SEVERITY_AXES; _SEV01 has no key 'broken_oracle' so returns 0.0
decide.py line 172: `has_override = any(_SEV01.get(attrs.get(a), 0.0) > 0.0 for a in _PLAN_HARD_OVERRIDE_AXES)` — same silent 0.0 return for new grade strings, override floor never fires

Plan says 'used for BOTH ... MAX contribution AND ... hard-override detection' but does not specify that _PLAN_SEVERITY_AXES and _PLAN_HARD_OVERRIDE_AXES iteration must dispatch to ORACLE_GRADE01 (not _SEV01) for the ac_unverifiable slot specifically
findings.finding: The plan defines ORACLE_GRADE01 for ac_unverifiable's 'MAX contribution' but does not specify that both internal _SEV01 usages in impact_plan must be replaced for ac_unverifiable: (1) the contribs list (`[_SEV01.get(attrs.get(a), 0.0) for a in _PLAN_SEVERITY_AXES]`) and (2) the has_override predicate (`any(_SEV01.get(attrs.get(a), 0.0) > 0.0 for a in _PLAN_HARD_OVERRIDE_AXES)`). New grade strings like 'broken_oracle' are absent from _SEV01, so both return 0.0 silently — defeating both the impact contribution and the floor trigger for missing_oracle/broken_oracle grades.
findings.location: What section (SCORING MECHANICS); src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py lines 166–172 (impact_plan)
scenarios: A verifier emits ac_unverifiable='broken_oracle'; impact_plan evaluates _SEV01.get('broken_oracle', 0.0)=0.0 in the contribs loop, so impact_sev=0.0 for that axis; has_override also returns False; the floor never fires — a broken-oracle finding scores 0.0 instead of ≥0.85, silently reversing the intent.

Unit tests for 'broken_oracle floors at >= 0.85' will catch this only if the test exercises the live code path — but the executor implementing decide.py without this spec note may write the wrong dispatch.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Scope bullet for decide.py, add: 'In impact_plan, replace the _SEV01 lookup for ac_unverifiable with ORACLE_GRADE01 in both (a) the contribs list comprehension over _PLAN_SEVERITY_AXES and (b) the has_override any() predicate over _PLAN_HARD_OVERRIDE_AXES; the other three override axes keep _SEV01 unchanged.' Also add a test: `assert impact_plan({'ac_unverifiable': 'broken_oracle'}) == 0.85` and `assert impact_plan({'ac_unverifiable': 'underspecified_oracle'}) == 0.55`.

findings.finding: `decide.impact_plan`'s hard-override detection (`has_override`) uses `_SEV01.get(attrs.get(a), 0.0) > 0.0`, where `_SEV01` is keyed on `{none, low, medium, high}`. The new grade values (`missing_oracle`, `broken_oracle`, `underspecified_oracle`) are NOT in `_SEV01`, so they all resolve to `0.0` — meaning the floor will NEVER fire for any of the new grades, silently defeating the core promise of the change.
findings.location: src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py — `impact_plan` / `_PLAN_HARD_OVERRIDE_AXES` / `_SEV01`
findings.suggested_fix: Replace or supplement `_SEV01.get(attrs.get(a), 0.0) > 0.0` with a set-membership check against the new grade vocabulary, e.g. `attrs.get(a) in {"missing_oracle", "broken_oracle"}` for floor-triggering grades and `attrs.get(a) == "underspecified_oracle"` for the capped contribution path; or define a separate `_ORACLE_GRADE_WEIGHT` mapping alongside `_SEV01`.

findings.finding: The plan replaces ac_unverifiable's grade vocabulary from {none, low, medium, high} to {none, underspecified_oracle, broken_oracle, missing_oracle}, but `impact_plan` in decide.py scores ALL seven plan-severity axes via the shared `_SEV01` map keyed on {none, low, medium, high}. The new grade strings are absent from `_SEV01`, so `_SEV01.get('underspecified_oracle', 0.0)` → 0.0, `_SEV01.get('broken_oracle', 0.0)` → 0.0, and `_SEV01.get('missing_oracle', 0.0)` → 0.0. This silently breaks BOTH the MAX-contribution leg (`impact_sev`) and the `has_override` predicate for all three new grades — `broken_oracle` and `missing_oracle` (which must floor at 0.85) would not trigger the floor, and none of the three grades would contribute to `impact_sev`. The plan states the floor logic is 'keyed on the ac_unverifiable grade value' but never specifies how the new string grades are mapped to numeric scores or how the existing `_SEV01`/`_PLAN_HARD_OVERRIDE_AXES` mechanism is adapted for the new vocabulary.
findings.location: Scope: src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py; cross-ref decide.py lines 48, 166-173 (_SEV01 / _PLAN_HARD_OVERRIDE_AXES / has_override logic)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope bullet for decide.py: 'Introduce a dedicated `_AC_UNVERIFIABLE_GRADE` lookup (e.g. `{"none": 0.0, "underspecified_oracle": 0.50, "broken_oracle": 1.0, "missing_oracle": 1.0}`) and update both the MAX-contribution leg and the `has_override` predicate to use it for `ac_unverifiable` specifically, leaving the `_SEV01` path unchanged for the other six axes. The `underspecified_oracle` numeric contribution (e.g. 0.50) must satisfy `min(0.50 * mult, ...) <= 0.55` under all amplifier settings so it can never reach the floor.'

findings.finding: The new oracle-kind grades (underspecified_oracle, broken_oracle, missing_oracle) are not in the existing `_SEV01` map in decide.py, yet `impact_plan` uses `_SEV01.get(attrs.get(a), 0.0)` to both compute the MAX contribution and detect the hard-override predicate. The plan specifies behavioral outcomes (missing/broken floor at 0.85; underspecified scores <=0.55) but never defines the numeric mapping or dispatch mechanism that achieves this — no new lookup table, no conditional branch, no sentinel value. An executor cannot derive the implementation without inventing the protocol.
findings.location: Scope: src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py + What section (grade definitions)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope entry for decide.py: 'Replace the `_SEV01` lookup for `ac_unverifiable` with a dedicated `_AC_UNVERIFIABLE_IMPACT` map: `{"missing_oracle": 1.0, "broken_oracle": 1.0, "underspecified_oracle": 0.55, "none": 0.0}`; the hard-override predicate checks `_AC_UNVERIFIABLE_IMPACT.get(attrs.get("ac_unverifiable"), 0.0) >= 0.85` (i.e., only missing/broken trigger it); `underspecified_oracle` contributes 0.55 to the MAX but never triggers the floor.'

evidence: 4 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
vague/subjective terms present: clean
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the explicit updated guard predicate to the Scope bullet for workflow_ops.py: e.g. 'replace the non-none check with an allowlist check: clear only when the current grade is in {missing_oracle, underspecified_oracle}; leave broken_oracle untouched'.
evidence: Plan: 'enrich_operator_attested currently clears any non-none ac_unverifiable to none when attested evidence satisfies the criterion; under the new vocabulary it clears missing_oracle and underspecified_oracle ... but NOT broken_oracle'

workflow_ops.py line 115: `if attrs.get('ac_unverifiable') not in (None, '', 'none'):` — this is a 'non-none' guard; with new grade strings broken_oracle passes this condition and would be cleared, violating the asymmetry
Plan says asymmetry 'is pinned by test' but does not write the updated predicate, creating an under-specification that the test itself cannot cover if the implementation is wrong
findings.finding: The plan states `enrich_operator_attested` clears `missing_oracle` and `underspecified_oracle` on attestation but never clears `broken_oracle`, and says 'the asymmetry is pinned by test'. However, the current guard in workflow_ops.py (line 115) reads `if attrs.get('ac_unverifiable') not in (None, '', 'none')` — a blanket non-none check that would also clear `broken_oracle`. The plan does not specify what the updated guard predicate must be, leaving the executor to infer it.
findings.location: What section (OPERATOR-ATTESTED ENRICH); src/rebar/llm/plan_review/workflow_ops.py lines 115–116
scenarios: Executor writes `if attrs.get('ac_unverifiable') not in (None, '', 'none', 'broken_oracle')` — correct; or writes `if attrs.get('ac_unverifiable') in ('missing_oracle', 'underspecified_oracle')` — also correct. Without the plan stating which form, both are guesses.

Executor mistakenly keeps the existing blanket check; broken_oracle gets cleared on attestation; a factually-wrong stated command in a plan is treated as 'verified' by an operator attestation — exactly the unsafe case the plan's asymmetry was designed to prevent.
findings.suggested_fix: In workflow_ops.py Scope bullet, specify: 'Change the guard from `not in (None, "", "none")` to `in {"missing_oracle", "underspecified_oracle"}` so broken_oracle is never cleared by attestation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify whether `build_payload`'s `schema` string bumps to `'plan_review_result_v3'` alongside the `IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION` bump, AND update the `latest_review_result`/`all_review_results` schema guard to accept the new schema string in lockstep — then assert the post-rollback schema state: rolled-back readers encountering plan-v3 sidecars must either safely skip them (via schema guard) or correctly degrade (the plan should pick one and pin it with a test).
evidence: Plan BACK-COMPAT: 'already-stored plan-v2 sidecars keep their legacy {low,medium,high} grades and are read as-is'
Plan ROLLBACK: 'reverting the change restores plan-v2 semantics and the corpora stay segmented; no expand-contract migration is needed because sidecars are immutable records'
sidecar.py line 149-152: schema guard accepts only 'plan_review_result_v1' or 'plan_review_result_v2'
sidecar.py line 480: build_payload hardcodes 'schema': 'plan_review_result_v2'
ABSENCE: plan does not address whether build_payload schema string bumps to 'plan_review_result_v3', nor how the reader guard is updated in lockstep

POST-ROLLBACK SCHEMA STATE: rolled-back reader encountering plan-v3-vocab sidecars with schema='plan_review_result_v2' would mis-score oracle-kind grades as unknown/zero because _SEV01 has no entry for 'underspecified_oracle'
findings.finding: The plan specifies that IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION bumps to plan-v3 but does not specify whether the sidecar schema string ('plan_review_result_v2' in build_payload) also bumps. The existing schema guard in latest_review_result/all_review_results accepts only 'plan_review_result_v1' or 'plan_review_result_v2'. If the schema string stays 'plan_review_result_v2', plan-v3 sidecars (with new oracle-kind grade strings) pass the guard and can be fed to rolled-back or mixed-version readers that don't understand the new vocabulary, silently mis-scoring the recall path. If the schema string bumps to 'plan_review_result_v3', the guard silently skips every new sidecar, breaking the recall/novelty mechanism entirely for all reviews after the change. The plan asserts rollback restores plan-v2 semantics but does not assert the post-rollback schema state for sidecars written during the plan-v3 window.
findings.location: Scope: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/sidecar.py / BACK-COMPAT section / ROLLBACK section
scenarios: Plan-v3 code is deployed; new sidecars are written with new oracle-kind grades. Schema string stays 'plan_review_result_v2'. Code is rolled back. Rolled-back impact_plan reads 'underspecified_oracle' via _SEV01 → gets 0.0 (missing key default) instead of the override floor 0.85, silently under-scoring findings that should floor.

Schema string bumps to 'plan_review_result_v3'. latest_review_result schema guard skips all new sidecars. Recall/novelty sub-call treats every re-review as 'no prior findings', defeating the recall mechanism introduced in story disused-unpoliced-solenodon.
findings.suggested_fix: Bump the `schema` string to `'plan_review_result_v3'` in `build_payload` (parallel to the `IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION` bump), add `'plan_review_result_v3'` to the schema guard allow-list in both `latest_review_result` and `all_review_results`, and add `'plan_review_result_v3'` to the schema-guard assertion in `test_sidecar_reader.py`. On rollback, the guard simply won't accept v3 sidecars (they are skipped), which is the safe degradation — document this explicitly in the ROLLBACK section so the post-rollback schema state is unambiguous.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a Scope bullet (or extend the sidecar.py bullet) explicitly naming the module responsible for write-time closed-set validation of the ac_unverifiable grade, and clarify whether this is enforced at the LLM structured-output layer, the application layer (e.g. sidecar.py), or both.
evidence: "the plan-review verification output schema accepts exactly {none, underspecified_oracle, broken_oracle, missing_oracle} for the axis and rejects anything else at write time" (Scope, verifier prompts bullet)
"IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION bump to plan-v3; the grade persists in verification.severity_attributes as today (values change, key stays)" (Scope, sidecar.py bullet — no mention of validation logic)
AC1 pins 'a rejection test for an out-of-vocabulary grade' but no Scope file is designated as the guard's owner
findings.finding: The Scope does not assign write-time closed-set schema validation to a specific file. The plan states 'the plan-review verification output schema...rejects anything else at write time' in the verifier-prompts bullet, but neither sidecar.py's Scope bullet nor any other Scope entry names the module that enforces the {none, underspecified_oracle, broken_oracle, missing_oracle} guard when a verification result is persisted. An implementer may add the validation to the prompt schema only (LLM structured-output enforcement) and omit an application-layer guard, or vice versa, leaving the AC1 rejection test with no clear home.
findings.location: Scope — plan_review_verifier.md / plan_review_verifier_agentic.md bullet; Scope — sidecar.py bullet; AC1
scenarios: Implementer adds the enum constraint only to the LLM structured-output schema in the prompt, skipping an application-level guard in sidecar.py; if the LLM framework allows passthrough on schema errors the rejection test has nothing to exercise.
Implementer adds the guard to sidecar.py but the prompts omit the schema definition, producing inconsistent enforcement surfaces.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the sidecar.py Scope bullet: '…grade persists in verification.severity_attributes as today; sidecar.py enforces the closed set {none, underspecified_oracle, broken_oracle, missing_oracle} at write time and raises ValueError on an unrecognised grade (the rejection test lives here).' If structured-output schema enforcement in the prompt is the primary gate, say so explicitly and note that the application layer provides a belt-and-suspenders guard.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test in test_impact_model_versioning.py that loads a synthetic plan-v2 sidecar with legacy ac_unverifiable grade 'low' (and 'high') and asserts it reads without error and is scored via _SEV01 (not ORACLE_GRADE01), pinning the back-compat read-path alongside the version-bump assertion.
evidence: 'legacy plan-v2 records are read as-is (segmentation by version is the back-compat mechanism, asserted in the doc note)'
doc-note-only assertion — no test pin is named for the read-path back-compat claim
'tests/unit/test_impact_model_versioning.py (plan-v2 -> plan-v3 assertion)' — scope describes only the version bump assertion, not a round-trip read test for old-vocabulary grades
findings.finding: The plan-v2 back-compat claim ('legacy plan-v2 records are read as-is') is proved only by a doc note, not by a test. The scope lists test_impact_model_versioning.py for 'plan-v2 -> plan-v3 assertion' but does not include a test that loads a synthetic plan-v2 sidecar carrying legacy {low,medium,high} ac_unverifiable grades and asserts it (a) reads without error under new code and (b) is scored via _SEV01, not ORACLE_GRADE01.
findings.location: AC3 / Scope: tests/unit/test_impact_model_versioning.py
scenarios: New schema-validation code applied at read time (not just write time) would break old sidecars silently; the only stated guard is a documentation note rather than a test assertion.
findings.suggested_fix: In test_impact_model_versioning.py, add a parametrized case: construct a minimal sidecar dict with impact_model_version='plan-v2' and severity_attributes={'ac_unverifiable': 'low'}, pass it through the scoring entry-point, and assert the returned impact uses _SEV01 lookup (e.g. the numeric contribution matches the old low-grade value). This makes the doc-note claim executable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either derive the underspecified_oracle contribution in ORACLE_GRADE01 from a shared constant (e.g., `LOWEST_BLOCKING_THRESHOLD - epsilon`) or add a companion assertion/test that fails if the blocking threshold falls at or below 0.55, preventing silent recoupling.
evidence: 'ORACLE_GRADE01 = {none: 0.0, underspecified_oracle: 0.55, broken_oracle: 1.0, missing_oracle: 1.0}' hardcoded in decide.py
'below the 0.60 lowest blocking threshold in force after calibration 3' — the 0.55 value is explicitly pegged to a calibration-specific threshold
'calibration 4 scope' — further threshold evolution is anticipated
findings.finding: The ORACLE_GRADE01 threshold value 0.55 (underspecified_oracle contribution) is hardcoded in decide.py. It is defined relative to the 'lowest blocking threshold in force after calibration 3' (0.60), meaning it will silently become incorrect if calibration 4 or later adjusts the blocking thresholds without also updating decide.py.
findings.location: Scope — src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/decide.py; What section: 'maps to an impact contribution <= 0.55'
scenarios: Calibration 4 lowers the blocking threshold to 0.50; underspecified_oracle at 0.55 now auto-blocks again, defeating the purpose of this story, with no static check to catch the drift.
A developer adjusts the blocking threshold constant in one file without realising decide.py's ORACLE_GRADE01 map contains a dependent magic number.
findings.suggested_fix: Define a module-level constant `UNDERSPECIFIED_ORACLE_CEILING = LOWEST_BLOCKING_THRESHOLD - 0.05` and use it in ORACLE_GRADE01 rather than a literal 0.55; add a test asserting `ORACLE_GRADE01['underspecified_oracle'] < LOWEST_BLOCKING_THRESHOLD`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a unit test in test_impact_model_versioning.py that reads a synthetic plan-v2 sidecar with legacy ac_unverifiable grade values ('low', 'medium', 'high') under the new code and asserts (a) no schema-validation error is raised and (b) the record is scored with plan-v2 semantics (not new ORACLE_GRADE01 semantics), replacing the 'asserted in the doc note' back-compat guarantee with an executable pin.
evidence: AC3 text: 'legacy plan-v2 records are read as-is (segmentation by version is the back-compat mechanism, asserted in the doc note)' — coverage is a documentation assertion, not a test
The new closed-set schema validation rejects out-of-vocabulary grades at write time; the plan does not prescribe a test confirming it does NOT fire on read of a legacy 'low'/'medium'/'high' grade
Changed behavior: plan-v2 grades ('low', 'medium', 'high') must continue to be read without error or misscoring under the new code — no test pin prescribed for this path
findings.finding: The back-compat guarantee for legacy plan-v2 records (old grades 'low'/'medium'/'high' read as-is without error or misscoring) is explicitly deferred to a doc note ('asserted in the doc note') rather than a test pin, leaving the read path for old records without behavioral test coverage despite being a changed behavior.
findings.location: AC3: 'legacy plan-v2 records are read as-is (segmentation by version is the back-compat mechanism, asserted in the doc note)'; Scope: tests/unit/test_impact_model_versioning.py
scenarios: A legacy plan-v2 sidecar with ac_unverifiable='low' is loaded; if the new closed-set validation also fires on reads, the record would be rejected or misscored, breaking back-compat silently
A replay consumer reads mixed-version corpora; a regression in the version-routing path goes undetected because the only assertion is in a doc note
findings.suggested_fix: In test_impact_model_versioning.py, add a parametrized test: for each of {'low','medium','high'}, construct a minimal plan-v2 sidecar dict with impact_model_version='plan-v2' and ac_unverifiable=<grade>, pass it through the read/scoring path, and assert the result equals the expected plan-v2 score (not the ORACLE_GRADE01 value) and that no ValidationError is raised.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `tests/unit/_audit_page_helpers.py` to Scope (or note it is intentionally pinned to plan-v2 to test back-compat): update the hardcoded `'impact_model_version': 'plan-v2'` on line 100 to reference `sidecar.IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION` dynamically, or document why the literal is correct for the test's purpose.
evidence: tests/unit/_audit_page_helpers.py line 100: `'impact_model_version': 'plan-v2'`
Plan Scope names `tests/unit/test_impact_model_versioning.py` for the version assertion update, but does not name `_audit_page_helpers.py`.
The helper is a shared fixture builder imported across multiple audit-layer tests.
findings.finding: `tests/unit/_audit_page_helpers.py` hardcodes `"impact_model_version": "plan-v2"` in a shared helper used by audit/sidecar tests. After the `IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION` bump to `plan-v3`, newly-emitted sidecars will carry `plan-v3`, but this helper will continue injecting `plan-v2` into synthetic payloads — causing silent divergence in any test that uses the helper to construct a payload and then reads `impact_model_version` against the new constant.
findings.location: `tests/unit/_audit_page_helpers.py` line 100; plan Scope / Unit tests section
scenarios: A test using `_audit_page_helpers.emit_plan_pass(...)` to build a synthetic plan-v3-era payload and then asserting `payload['impact_model_version'] == sidecar.IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION` fails because the helper hardcodes the old string.
Calibration/replay consumers that segment by version would see audit-layer synthetic records tagged plan-v2 instead of plan-v3, contaminating the replay cohort.
findings.suggested_fix: Import `from rebar.llm.plan_review import sidecar as plan_sidecar` at the top of `_audit_page_helpers.py` and change line 100 to `'impact_model_version': plan_sidecar.IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or a smoke-test step) that runs at least one of the two updated verifier prompts against the live LLM on a known fixture and asserts that the returned ac_unverifiable value is one of {none, underspecified_oracle, broken_oracle, missing_oracle} and is accepted by the write-time schema — proving the prompt-to-grade pipeline end-to-end.
evidence: AC1 proving commands: 'acceptance test and a rejection test for an out-of-vocabulary grade' — these are schema/unit tests, not live verifier invocations
E6(f): 'when the plan CUTS OVER or DEFAULTS to a new code path, at least one acceptance criterion EXERCISES that path as it will run in production (e.g. end-to-end against the live model/dependency)'
The two prompt files are in scope and change the LLM output vocabulary — the LLM is a live/external boundary
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion exercises the updated verifier prompts end-to-end against the live LLM to confirm the new oracle-kind grade vocabulary is actually emitted. All proving commands are unit tests that inject grade strings directly into the scoring engine; the LLM-boundary hop (prompt → model → grade string → sidecar write) is never exercised as it will run in production.
findings.location: AC1 / Scope: plan_review_verifier.md + plan_review_verifier_agentic.md
scenarios: A prompt formatting error, a misplaced worked example, or a model that defaults to the old low/medium/high vocabulary would be caught only at schema-rejection time (an error) rather than confirmed by a passing green integration test; the plan's ACs cannot distinguish 'prompts produce the right grades' from 'prompts produce no recognisable grade at all'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a new ADR (or extend ADR 0036) documenting the oracle-kind taxonomy, the ORACLE_GRADE01/`_SEV01` split rationale, and the asymmetric attestation rule so calibration-4 authors have a durable design reference.
evidence: 'segmentation by impact_model_version (ADR 0036)' — ADR practice is live and cited
'the other three override axes (dod_uncertifiable, undecomposed, divergent_implementation) keep their ordinal vocabulary... calibration 4 scope' — future evolution anticipated
Scope lists only docs/plan-review-gate.md, not a new ADR
ORACLE_GRADE01 is a novel divergence from _SEV01 with design rationale (broken vs underspecified semantics) worth preserving
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new axis-specific scoring map (ORACLE_GRADE01 diverging from the shared _SEV01 pattern), a three-grade oracle-kind taxonomy, asymmetric attestation semantics (broken_oracle not cleared by attestation), and a model version bump — all architectural decisions — but plans no new ADR, only a gate-doc update. ADR 0036 is cited as the live precedent for version-segmentation decisions, making the absence of a companion ADR for the plan-v3 design rationale a gap future maintainers will feel when calibration 4 revisits the other axes.
findings.location: Scope / docs/plan-review-gate.md; Why section referencing ADR 0036
scenarios: A calibration-4 author needs to understand WHY broken_oracle keeps the floor while underspecified does not — without an ADR, they read code comments or reconstruct the reasoning from this story's prose.
A new team member sees ORACLE_GRADE01 and _SEV01 coexisting in decide.py and cannot find the documented rationale for having two maps.
findings.suggested_fix: Create docs/adr/NNNN-plan-v3-oracle-kind-grades.md covering: (1) motivation from calibration-3 data, (2) why broken_oracle keeps the floor while underspecified_oracle does not, (3) why broken_oracle is not cleared by attestation, (4) the ORACLE_GRADE01 map values and their derivation, (5) relationship to ADR 0036 versioning strategy.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a staged rollout path (e.g., a feature flag or shadow-scoring mode that runs both v2 and v3 scoring in parallel for an initial traffic slice) so the calibration of the new grade mapping can be validated against live data before full cut-over.
evidence: No mention of a feature flag or canary anywhere in the plan.
'IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION bumps plan-v2 -> plan-v3 in the same change' — the version bump controls sidecar segmentation, not traffic percentage.
AC line 2 pins the scoring change as immediate: 'underspecified_oracle findings score <= 0.55 … and never trigger the hard floor'
The plan's own rollback text addresses revert, not staged rollout: 'reverting the change restores plan-v2 semantics'
findings.finding: The plan changes live scoring behavior (underspecified_oracle findings that previously auto-blocked will no longer block) for 100% of new plan-v3 verifications the moment the code is deployed, with no feature flag, canary, or staged rollout path described.
findings.location: ## What — scoring mechanics paragraph; ## Scope — decide.py entry; AC line 2
scenarios: If the new grade mapping has an off-by-one or the verifier LLM over-produces underspecified_oracle grades, a wave of findings that should block will silently not block before operators notice and revert.
A gradual canary (e.g., shadow-score a percentage of reviews under both models before switching) would surface calibration drift without impacting real gate decisions.
findings.suggested_fix: Introduce a boolean flag (e.g., ORACLE_GRADE_V3_ENABLED, defaulting to False) in the gate config. When False, the old _SEV01 path is used for ac_unverifiable; when True, ORACLE_GRADE01 is used. Flip it to True after shadow-scoring a representative sample (e.g., 5% of reviews for one sprint) shows no unexpected drop in blocking rate beyond the calibration-3-predicted 35.5%.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the Scope or Back-compat section that the sidecar payload `schema` string stays at `'plan_review_result_v2'` (the `IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION` tag is the segmentation key, not the schema string), and confirm that `latest_review_result` requires no update — or, if the schema string IS bumped, that `latest_review_result` must add `'plan_review_result_v3'` to its acceptance guard.
evidence: Plan: 'IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION bumps plan-v2 -> plan-v3 in the same change'
sidecar.py line 480: `'schema': 'plan_review_result_v2'` (hard-coded in build_payload)
sidecar.py lines 149-152: `latest_review_result` accepts only `plan_review_result_v1` or `plan_review_result_v2`; a plan_review_result_v3 would be rejected
ABSENCE: the plan says nothing about whether the schema string should stay at v2 or bump to v3
findings.finding: The plan bumps IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION to 'plan-v3' but does not specify whether the sidecar payload schema string (currently hard-coded as `"plan_review_result_v2"` in `build_payload`) should be kept or bumped. The `latest_review_result` reader explicitly guards `payload.get('schema') in ("plan_review_result_v1", "plan_review_result_v2")`. If an implementer reasonably bumps the schema string to `plan_review_result_v3` (the payload now contains a different ac_unverifiable vocabulary), every new plan-v3 sidecar is rejected by the reader, silently breaking the recall/novelty sub-call for all subsequent reviews — carryover findings score novelty=0.0 (permanent carryover) without any error.
findings.location: Scope / sidecar.py + Back-compat section
scenarios: Implementer bumps schema string to plan_review_result_v3; all new sidecars are silently rejected by latest_review_result; novelty sub-call receives no prior findings; all findings score novelty=0.0 (carryover); no findings are ever dropped by the rising floor — the novelty mechanism is defeated without any error signal.

Implementer leaves schema string at plan_review_result_v2; reader continues to accept new sidecars; legacy plan-v2 sidecars with old ac_unverifiable vocabulary are also accepted — but this is correct because the segmentation-by-impact_model_version already handles that.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Back-compat section: 'The sidecar payload `schema` string remains `plan_review_result_v2` — the `impact_model_version` field is the segmentation key (ADR 0036); bumping the schema string is NOT part of this change and would require updating the `latest_review_result` reader's acceptance guard.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a one-sentence architectural note to the Scope (decide.py) stating whether ORACLE_GRADE01 is the intended extension mechanism for future per-axis grade vocabularies, or whether a more general axis-local-map dispatch (e.g. `_AXIS_MAPS: dict[str, dict] = {'ac_unverifiable': ORACLE_GRADE01, ...}` defaulting to `_SEV01`) should be introduced now to avoid repeated ad-hoc branching when calibration 4 examines the other override axes.
evidence: Plan states: 'ORACLE_GRADE01 = {none: 0.0, underspecified_oracle: 0.55, broken_oracle: 1.0, missing_oracle: 1.0} — used for BOTH the ac_unverifiable MAX contribution AND the hard-override detection'

Plan states: 'The other three override axes (dod_uncertifiable, undecomposed, divergent_implementation) keep their ordinal vocabulary and any-non-none floor, untouched in this story (their grade mix is unmeasured; calibration 4 scope)'

Existing code: decide.py line 166 uses a single list comprehension `[_SEV01.get(attrs.get(a), 0.0) for a in _PLAN_SEVERITY_AXES]` uniformly over all seven axes — the plan requires ac_unverifiable to branch away from this into ORACLE_GRADE01, establishing a precedent for per-axis special-casing that the other override axes may later need
No mention in plan or ADR 0036 of a per-axis map extension convention
findings.finding: The plan introduces ORACLE_GRADE01 as the first axis-specific scoring map that diverges from the universal _SEV01 ordinal pattern, with no documented extension policy for applying the same treatment to the other three override axes (dod_uncertifiable, undecomposed, divergent_implementation) if calibration 4 finds a similar grade-mix problem there. The plan explicitly defers those axes to 'calibration 4 scope' but does not establish whether ORACLE_GRADE01 is the intended reusable per-axis mechanism or a one-off special-case, leaving the next maintainer without a clear precedent.
findings.location: What section — 'SCORING MECHANICS' paragraph; Scope — decide.py
scenarios: Calibration 4 finds that dod_uncertifiable has a similar grade-mix problem (e.g. 'mentioned but incomplete' vs 'factually wrong DoD') and a second implementer must decide independently whether to add a SECOND axis-specific map or extend ORACLE_GRADE01 — without a stated policy, both choices are defensible and the models diverge
The list-comprehension in impact_plan is extended for ac_unverifiable via an if-branch; future axes each add another branch, making the function progressively harder to reason about

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a grade-classification protocol to the verifier prompt specification (in the plan's Scope bullet for the .md files) — at minimum a priority rule such as 'prefer underspecified_oracle when any verification method is constructible; use broken_oracle only when the plan's OWN stated command/path/symbol is demonstrably wrong; reserve missing_oracle for criteria that are inherently unobservable regardless of how specified' — co-located with the three-grade definitions.
evidence: Plan: 'BOTH verifier prompts define the three grades with one worked example each drawn from the calibration-3 classified sample'

Current verifier prompt ac_unverifiable description is a single sentence with none|low|medium|high — the new prompt must convey three new semantic grades to the LLM with enough differentiation to reproduce the 56%/37%/7% split reliably

Plan defines the grades in the 'What' section but these definitions are plan metadata, not embedded in the prompt specification — no cross-reference or protocol for 'when in doubt, prefer underspecified_oracle over broken_oracle' is stated
Absence: no decision tree, priority ordering, or tiebreaker rule is specified anywhere in the plan for the verifier's grade-assignment decision
findings.finding: The plan instructs that both verifier prompts define each grade 'with one worked example each drawn from the calibration-3 classified sample', but provides no decision protocol or differentiating criterion for the verifier to classify among the three grades. The boundary between underspecified_oracle ('a check exists or is clearly constructible; the finding demands the exact command/file/value') and broken_oracle ('a stated proving command/symbol/count is factually wrong') and missing_oracle ('no verification method exists or could exist') is non-obvious: a verifier LLM receiving only three one-sentence definitions plus one example each may misclassify at the boundary cases (e.g. a partially-correct command vs a structurally correct but value-wrong command). No classification rule or tiebreaker is co-located with the schema the prompts define.
findings.location: What section (verifier prompt change requirement); src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_verifier.md and plan_review_verifier_agentic.md (current ac_unverifiable definition, lines 67-68 / 73-74)
scenarios: A verifier sees 'the plan says run pytest tests/unit/foo.py but that file does not exist in the repo' — is this broken_oracle (stated command fails) or missing_oracle (no verifiable method)? Without a tiebreaker the verifier may flip between grades across runs.

A verifier sees 'the AC says the feature is tested but no test file is named' — is this underspecified_oracle (a test clearly could exist, path not given) or missing_oracle? The 7%/56% boundary is exactly this ambiguity.

findings.finding: `src/rebar/llm/plan_review/workflow_ops.py:enrich_operator_attested` hard-codes the guard `attrs.get('ac_unverifiable') not in (None, '', 'none')` and clears the axis to `'none'`. After this change all three new grade values (`missing_oracle`, `broken_oracle`, `underspecified_oracle`) are non-none and will be unconditionally cleared to `'none'` for any operator-attested AC — including `missing_oracle` and `broken_oracle`, which are supposed to keep the hard floor. This file is not in the plan's Scope.
findings.location: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/workflow_ops.py lines 113–116 (`enrich_operator_attested`)

findings.finding: Both verifier prompt files (plan_review_verifier.md and plan_review_verifier_agentic.md) currently define the `ac_unverifiable` axis with the old `none|low|medium|high` vocabulary and the rule 'HARD-OVERRIDE axis: any non-none marks the finding auto-high.' The plan scopes updating the verify rubric prompt but does NOT specify that BOTH prompt files must be updated — only 'The plan-review verification output schema + verify rubric prompt' is listed. The agentic variant is a separate file with the same stale vocabulary and conflicting rule.
findings.location: Scope section: 'The plan-review verification output schema + verify rubric prompt'; src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_verifier_agentic.md:73-74

findings.finding: The `enrich_operator_attested` function in workflow_ops.py clears `ac_unverifiable` to 'none' whenever its value is `not in (None, '', 'none')`. Under the new enum, a `broken_oracle` grade (meaning the stated verification command is factually wrong — a real plan defect, not an observability limitation) would be cleared by the operator-attested enrichment path, silently suppressing a genuine defect signal. The plan does not specify how `enrich_operator_attested` should behave with the new grade vocabulary.
findings.location: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/workflow_ops.py:115-116 (enrich_operator_attested decision rule)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to scope: 'Update `enrich_operator_attested` clearing predicate to protect hard-floor grades: only clear `ac_unverifiable` when its value is `underspecified_oracle` (not `broken_oracle` or `missing_oracle`, which are real plan defects independent of observability).'

findings.finding: The plan's Scope says 'The plan-review verification output schema + verify rubric prompt' will be updated, but the two concrete prompt files that define `ac_unverifiable` with the old `none|low|medium|high` vocabulary (`src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_verifier.md` and `src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_verifier_agentic.md`) are not named. It is ambiguous whether both are intended edit targets.
findings.location: Scope — 'The plan-review verification output schema + verify rubric prompt'; src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_verifier.md line 67; src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_verifier_agentic.md line 73

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
